abstract.t: This document specifies Ten64, a novel number serialization format representing Text Encoded Numbers as a Base-64 binary stream. Similar to how hexadecimal uses characters 0-9 and a-f to represent base-16 data, Ten64 expands on this concept by using a virtual binary alphabet of 0-9, a-z, A-Z, and the "@" symbol to represent base-64 numeric data.

Using big-ending (aka network ordering), Ten64 is designed to optimize the reading, writing, and streaming of numbers. It uses math-safe characters to support safe embedding in programming languages and addresses transit precision issues commonly found in ubiquitous data interchange formats like JSON or XML.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: As computer systems scale in both volume and streaming throughput, standard text-representation formats like Extensible Markup Language (XML) and JavaScript Object Notation (JSON) often introduce precision loss during transit. This is particularly prevalent due to double-precision floating-point constraints. To solve these and related issues, Ten64 is introduced.

Alternative notations and bases have historically been explored to improve human-machine interfaces, ranging from early discussions on binary notations to modern concepts like Hexadecimal , and Bioctal . Ten64 builds on this history by utilizing a 64-character virtual binary alphabet composed of standard ASCII-7 / UTF-8 characters . By avoiding standard mathematical symbols, Ten64 allows exact numbers to be embedded directly into programming environments without compiler confusion.

section.name: Requirements Language
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.
This document aligns with the formal IETF standardization procedures defined by the Internet Standards Process in BCP 9 .

section.name: Special Characters
section.t: Beyond the primary alphabet, Ten64 relies on explicit syntactic characters to organize binary streams and chunks.

dl.dd: OPTIONAL explicit beginning of a Ten64 binary stream / chunk.
The Decimal or Number Space Separator.
OPTIONAL explicit end of a Ten64 number or number list.
Any whitespace characters (Line Feeds, Tabs, Spaces, Return Sequences) MAY be included and MUST be ignored during evaluation.

section.name: The Ten64 Alphabet
section.t: The primary Ten64 alphabet acts as a big-endian virtual binary system. Unlike the encodings defined in , each valid character maps to a specific 6-bit binary chunk and an Arabic numeral value (0-63). All virtual binary sequences are interpreted as integers.

section.name: Segmented Numbers and Use Cases
section.t: A core feature of Ten64 is the "Segmented Number" representing a list of integers delimited by periods. E.g., "#0.1.2;" expands to integers 0, 1, and 2.

section.name: BigDecimals
section.t: Ten64 easily represents arbitrarily precise BigDecimals by encoding the EXACT number of decimal places. E.g., "#-1.8;" expands to -1 and 8 (which contextually translates to the decimal -1.8). This mitigates the floating-point truncation issues observed in JSON.

section.name: Dates, Datetimes, and Timestamps
section.t: Ten64 uses Segmented Numbers to heavily condense date formats. While provides standard string representations, Ten64 condenses these logically:

ul.li: Dates: Encoded as the year followed by a dot, one char for the month, and one for the day (e.g., "#Vv.21;" expands to 2023-02-01).
Datetimes: Timezone, hour, and minute each require one char appended to the date (e.g., "#Vv.216jR;" expands to 2023-02-01 CST 7:53 PM).
MiliTimestamps: Appends a char for seconds and uses a dot for milliseconds (e.g., "#Vv.216jR12;").
NanoTimestamps: Appends additional characters representing nanoseconds not tracked as milliseconds.

section.name: Lists and Geometric Points
section.t: Ten64 supports 1D lists separated by commas (e.g., "#1,2,3,4;") and N-dimensional points combined with decimal segmentation (e.g., "#1.2.3,4.5.6;" expands to a list of 3D points).

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: Adligo Inc. maintains a Private Enterprise Number (PEN) Object Identifier (OID) registered with IANA. The specific OID allocated for the Ten64 serialization format is 1.3.6.1.4.1.33097.8.1.
Further documentation regarding this registration is available at: https://adligo.github.io/papers.adligo.com/ietf-rfcs/Ten64.html
There are no other IANA considerations for this document.

front.title: Key words for use in RFCs to Indicate Requirement Levels
UTF-8, a transformation format of ISO 10646
Ambiguity of Uppercase vs Lowercase in RFC 2119
Letters to the editor: On binary notation
The Base16, Base32, and Base64 Data Encodings
